title: Notifying Professional Contacts: Doctors, Accountants, Attorneys
summary: Professional contacts are easy to overlook amid bigger notifications, but reaching out to them closes out records and can surface useful information.
category: After a Loss II: Notifications & Accounts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Notifying Professional Contacts: Doctors, Accountants, Attorneys | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Professional contacts are easy to overlook amid bigger notifications, but reaching out to them closes out records and can surface useful information.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Doctors' offices need to close out records and cancel future appointments.
An accountant may hold useful financial history.
An attorney may hold estate documents you don't yet have copies of.

paragraphs: Notifying the person's doctors closes out their medical file and cancels any upcoming appointments — a small task, but one that avoids confusing reminder calls later.

An accountant or financial advisor may have useful historical records, and an attorney may be holding original estate documents (wills, trusts) that the family doesn't yet have copies of — worth reaching out even if you're not sure they were actively working with the person.

nextSteps: Contact any known doctors to close records and cancel appointments.
Reach out to an accountant or financial advisor, if one existed.
Ask an attorney of record whether they're holding original documents.
disclaimer: General guidance — not every family will have all of these professional relationships to notify.
